sanskrit_devanagari:
अन्नाद्भ‍वन्ति भूतानि पर्जन्यादन्नसम्भवः ।
यज्ञा‍द्भ‍‍वति पर्जन्यो यज्ञः कर्मसमुद्भ‍वः ॥ १४ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
annād bhavanti bhūtāni parjanyād anna-sambhavaḥ |
yajñād bhavati parjanyo yajñaḥ karma-samudbhavaḥ || 14 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: All living bodies subsist on food grains, which are produced from rains. Rains are produced by performance of yajña [sacrifice], and yajña is born of prescribed duties.
All living bodies subsist on food grains, which are produced from rains. Rains are produced by performance of yajna [sacrifice], and yajna is born of prescribed duties.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 3116 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Śrīla Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa, a great commentator on the Bhagavad-gītā, writes as follows: ye indrādy-aṅgatayāvasthitaṁ yajñaṁ sarveśvaraṁ viṣṇum abhyarcya tac-cheṣam aśnanti tena tad deha-yātrāṁ sampādayanti, te santaḥ sarveśvarasya yajña-puruṣasya bhaktāḥ sarva-kilbiṣair anādi-kāla-vivṛddhair ātmān…

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.14 It is a matter of direct perception that annat, from food, which is eaten and is transformed into blood and semen; bhavanti, are born; bhutani, the creatures. Anna-sambhavah, the origin of food; is parjanyat, from rainfall. Parjanyah, rainfall; bhavati, originates; from yajnat, from sacrifice.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-3-14-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: The Advaita reading grounds the chain in Vedic cosmology, citing Manu 6.76: oblations properly poured into fire reach the sun; from the sun comes rain; from rain comes food; from food, beings. The chain is metaphysically real, not metaphor. yajñaḥ karma-samudbhavaḥ — yajña arises from karma (the action of performing the rite). The verse's cosmological reach is large; engineering analogs must register honestly that the analog is a pale shadow of the original cosmic claim.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.14 From food arise all beings; from rain food is produced. These two facts are matters of common experience. ‘From sacrifice comes rain’ this is known from the scriptures such as, ‘The oblations offered in fire reach the sun, and from the sun comes rain’ (Manu, 6.76), and sacrifice is born out of…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-3-14-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: The Vishishtadvaita reading: the chain at the operational layer: from food arise beings; from rain food is produced (matters of common experience). From sacrifice comes rain (per Manu 6.76). yajñaḥ karma-samudbhavaḥ — sacrifice is born of action (the agent's act of collecting materials, etc.). karma in turn arises from brahman (the body / Prakṛti). The chain is metaphysically real but the operational reading is kept visible.

positions.rendering: yajña is born of prescribed duties
sacrifice is produced by the performance of prescribed duties
yajña arises from karma (action); citing Manu 6.76 — oblations into fire reach the sun, from sun comes rain, from rain comes food
yajña is born of the activities of collecting materials etc. by the agent; activity arises from Brahman / the body born of Prakṛti

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All four traditions preserve the chain: anna → bhūta → vṛṣṭi → yajña → karma. The verse states a metaphysical-cosmological loop. The Advaita reading cites Manu directly to show the chain is real (oblations literally produce rain). The Vishishtadvaita reading takes karma here as the activity of collecting materials and performing the rite, with brahma in 3.15 then naming the Prakṛti from which activity arises. Engineering analogs to this verse are inherently STRETCHED: the original is cosmological, not metaphor; the engineering layer can register a sustaining loop honestly only at the operational layer.

literal_meaning: Beings come into being from food; food comes from rain; rain comes from yajña; yajña is born of action.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 3.14 begins the cosmic chain. annād bhavanti bhūtāni — beings come from food; parjanyād anna-sambhavaḥ — food's origin is rain; yajñād bhavati parjanyaḥ — rain comes from yajña; yajñaḥ karma-samudbhavaḥ — yajña is born of action. Per the Advaita reading (citing Manu 6.76): oblations poured into fire reach the sun, from the sun comes rain, from rain comes food. Per the Vishishtadvaita reading: the chain is metaphysically real; yajña and the prescribed actions that produce it are required for the chain to remain intact. The verse names the loop: action → yajña → rain → food → beings → action.
